food_subgroup: Red meat — lamb, grilled or pan-seared steak cut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Turkish-style marinated lamb steak entry; closest parent 'lamb steak (food product)' used as the best available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat of sheep (lamb), fresh or chilled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fresh/chilled sheep meat; no specific grilled lamb steak code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227197003 refers to lamb meat as a substance; no specific steak preparation code available. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of lamb steak is approximately 200 g, representing one steak cut from the leg or loin, grilled or pan-seared and served with accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, leg, whole (shank and sirloin), separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, choice, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #17082
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb leg (separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, choice, roasted) used as the closest approved source for a grilled Turkish lamb steak. Values represent cooked lamb with moderate fat content. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical Turkish marinade seasoning (salt, spices). No exact Turkish-style lamb steak entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this food.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure cooked meat contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0.0 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently classify all plain cooked meats, including lamb, as having a glycemic index of effectively 0 because they contain no glycemic carbohydrates. GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) per convention for zero-carbohydrate foods; gi_category is 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0 g per 100 g and serving carbohydrate is 0 g.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving of Turkish lamb steak provides approximately 640 mg sodium from marinade salt and spices, representing about 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb steak contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of effectively 1, making it a suitable high-protein, low-GI protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments and in portions that respect total energy targets.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 200 g serving delivers approximately 9.2 g saturated fat and 190 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat intake to lean cuts, trim visible fat, and keep lamb steak consumption to no more than 1–2 times per week in line with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb steak is a thick cut of lamb meat, typically taken from the leg or loin, that is marinated in a blend of olive oil, garlic, lemon juice, cumin, paprika, black pepper, salt, and fresh herbs such as rosemary or thyme before being grilled over high heat or pan-seared to achieve a caramelised crust and a juicy, tender interior. In Turkish cuisine, lamb is the most prized red meat and lamb steak (kuzu bifteği) is a centrepiece dish served in traditional Turkish and Ottoman-style restaurants. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and slightly smoky with herbal and citrus notes from the marinade. Nutritionally, lamb steak is a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food providing approximately 26 g protein and 217 kcal per 100 g, along with meaningful amounts of iron (2.0 mg), zinc (4.5 mg), vitamin B12 (2.7 mcg), and selenium (26 mcg). The fat content is moderate at 12 g per 100 g, of which 4.6 g is saturated fat. Because it contains no glycemic carbohydrate, it has a glycemic index of effectively 1 and is suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. Clinically, sodium from marinade seasoning warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, and saturated fat content is relevant for cardiovascular disease risk management. In Singapore, Turkish lamb steak is served in Turkish and Lebanese restaurants and is positioned as a premium dining option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lamb steak?
Is lamb steak good for diabetics?
Is lamb steak high in protein?
How much sodium is in a lamb steak?
What nutrients does lamb steak have?
Is lamb steak high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: No exact Turkish-style lamb steak entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb leg data, which is the highest-priority approved source available. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical Turkish marinade seasoning. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source is an adaptation rather than an exact match.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked lamb meat, which contains no digestible carbohydrate. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero as lamb meat contains no sugars. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero as meat contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as cooked lamb meat contains no vitamin C. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry used as the primary source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1: not reported in the primary source entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1: not reported in the primary source entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1: not reported in the primary source entry. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) because lamb steak contains 0.0 g digestible carbohydrate per 100 g; the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature classify all plain cooked meats as having no glycemic response; gi_category is 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because serving carbohydrate is 0 g. 4. Sodium value of 320 mg per 100 g is an upward adaptation from the USDA base value (~65 mg per 100 g for unseasoned roasted lamb leg) to reflect typical Turkish restaurant marinade seasoning with salt, cumin, paprika, and garlic; this is documented as an adaptation in nutrient_source. 5. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311349 is used as the closest parent; no exact Turkish marinated lamb steak code exists in FoodOn. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC (sheep meat, fresh or chilled) is the closest parent; no grilled lamb steak-specific FoodEx2 code exists. 7. SNOMED CT 227197003 (lamb meat substance) is the closest parent; no preparation-specific code exists. 8. This food is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine restaurants in Singapore at SGD 33.90 per portion.
